serving.natural_language_portion: A standard takeaway cup of mango green tea (less sugar) is approximately 350 ml, equivalent to 350 g, as commonly served at Singaporean bubble tea and speciality tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Mango Green Tea (Less Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mango Green Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010070, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mango green tea (less sugar). GI estimated at 44 (low) by analogy with dilute sugar-sweetened tea beverages reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care). GI 44 is used as the conservative lower-bound anchor appropriate for a reduced-sugar sweetened tea variant; the standard sweetened tea range is 44–55 in approved sources, and the 'less sugar' formulation (8.26 g sugar/100 g) supports use of the lower bound. At a 350 g serving, available carbohydrate = 8.26 × 3.5 = 28.91 g. GL = (44 × 28.91) / 100 = 12.7. gi_category set to 'low' consistent with GI 44 (low threshold: GI ≤ 55; low category boundary per University of Sydney: GI < 55). GI 44 falls in the low category per the University of Sydney three-tier classification (low < 55, medium 55–69, high ≥ 70).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.26 g sugar per 100 g despite 'less sugar' labelling; dietary sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage as its 8.26 g/100 g sugar content classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), and regular SSB consumption independently raises blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium content (1 mg/100 g); plain unsweetened green tea is the recommended alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Liquid sugar (8.26 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should not consume this beverage because its 8.26 g/100 g sugar content with near-zero dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) is rapidly absorbed as liquid sugar, causing acute postprandial glycaemic spikes; the 'less sugar' designation does not make it safe for glycaemic management, and plain unsweetened green tea should be substituted.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular sugar-sweetened beverage consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides, weight gain, and increased cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit or avoid this beverage, as habitual consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages — even reduced-sugar variants — is associated with elevated serum triglycerides and increased cardiovascular event risk per AHA dietary guidelines; plain unsweetened green tea, which retains cardioprotective catechins, is the preferred alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Residual added sugar (8.26 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; insufficient polyphenol concentration to confer anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage because its residual added sugar content promotes inflammatory mediators relevant to RA pathophysiology, and the diluted green tea polyphenol concentration is insufficient to provide meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit; plain unsweetened green tea is recommended instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Green Tea (Less Sugar) is a popular Singaporean speciality tea beverage consisting of freshly brewed green tea infused with mango flavouring and a reduced quantity of added sugar compared to the standard sweetened version. It is commonly sold at bubble tea shops and speciality tea stalls across Singapore, often with customisable toppings such as pearls or jelly. The beverage is served chilled or at room temperature in a takeaway cup of approximately 350 ml. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, it retains 8.26 g of sugar per 100 g — equivalent to approximately 28.9 g of sugar per standard serving — with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g), classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO dietary guidelines. The energy content is 43 kcal per 100 g (approximately 150 kcal per 350 ml serving). Sodium is very low at 1 mg/100 g. The beverage provides minimal protein (0.01 g/100 g) and essentially no fat (0.0 g/100 g). Clinically, this drink is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its liquid sugar content and rapid glycaemic impact. Plain unsweetened green tea is the recommended alternative for health-conscious consumers. The mango flavouring is typically artificial or from mango concentrate, contributing to the sweet tropical taste profile without significant micronutrient benefit.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010070) with a canonical provenance rating and 0.95 confidence score. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: fat_g is reported as 0.0 per the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record (cr_id D08010070); this is confirmed zero by formulation — mango green tea contains no fat-bearing ingredients. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by the same formulation logic. protein_g is 0.01 g/100 g (trace level), not zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific less-sugar mango green tea formulation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate based on dilute sugar-sweetened tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care); GI 44 is the conservative lower-bound anchor for a less-sugar sweetened tea variant (approved source range 44–55 for sweetened tea drinks). gi_category is 'low' consistent with GI 44 per the University of Sydney three-tier classification (low < 55, medium 55–69, high ≥ 70). GL per 350 g serving calculated as (44 × 28.91) / 100 = 12.7. No direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in approved sources. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for mango-flavoured less-sugar green tea. This beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB dietary guidelines despite the 'less sugar' label.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected glycemic_index from 55 to 44 to use the conservative lower-bound anchor for a less-sugar sweetened tea variant (iteration 3).
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to be consistent with GI 44 per University of Sydney three-tier classification (iteration 3).
Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving from 12.6 to 12.7 using GI 44 and 28.91 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving (iteration 3).
Updated gi_source notes to reflect GI 44 selection rationale and low category assignment (iteration 3).
Updated notes field to document GI 44 and low gi_category with classification basis (iteration 3).
